title: Intro to Experimental Design
content_objectives: ["Understand test logistics", "Learn about test sections/format", "Learn about available resources"]
content_resources: [{"description": "", "type": "reading", "title": "Introduction", "url": "https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nP4GjcruPCxOhDJ22YrJADAm8iKkvT3nCvKFElWGKLY/edit?usp=sharing"}]
systemPrompt:
Test responders by making a series of questions based on the attached google document. For reference, here is the text of the google document:

Introduction to Experimental Design
WELCOME TO EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN! Or, what we like to call EXPD. In this module, you will learn about what this event is, and how it runs. 

Please be sure to click on the links included in this module to at least familiarize yourself with the event resources before the start of the season. 

Please feel free to use the commenting feature to ask any questions you might have while reading through. This will be a lot of reading, but bare with us!

Basics
EXPD is a lot more about understanding the rubric, and applying skills rather than memorizing facts. 
Event Logistics: 3 partners. 50 minutes. This information is typically what you’ll be given during an invitational/competition (at regionals it’s just materials, no background information). With provided materials, you will come up with a small experiment on the spot, and will be responsible for recording all parts of the planning, execution, results, analysis, etc… You’ll learn about the specifics of what to write in later modules. 

Everything you do during the test will be recorded on a student report form. This is your entire test paper. It’s where you write the hypothesis, procedure, data table, graph, conclusion, etc. 
The different lettered sections on the student report form are graded based on a checklist. This checklist is SO important to doing well on the test. It tells you the criteria for each section (exactly what the graders are looking for) so you should basically memorize the rubric, maximize your points to the best of your ability, and make the grader’s life easy. Here is a longer, VERY HELPFUL document that breaks down the rubric criteria even MORE and tells you exactly what you need to score points. We will be doing practice throughout the year so don’t worry about memorizing every single bullet right now (though it would be helpful). 

The sections of the test
As you saw in the student report form and the test rubric, there are two parts to the test:
Part 1: Design & Construction of the Experiment (20 minutes)
Part 2: Data, Analysis, and Conclusions (30 minutes)
You will receive the Part 2 papers after 20 minutes. Your Part 1 papers will not be taken away, so you can still work on them, but you should divide up your time appropriately. 
	
There are subsections under each part, lettered from A to P which you can see on the report form or the checklist. Here they are for your convenience, but specifics will be in those documents and later modules!

Part 1
A. Statement of the Problem
B. Hypothesis
C. Variables
D. Experimental Control (Standard of Comparison)
E. Materials
F. Procedure and Set-up Diagrams
G. Qualitative Observations
H. Quantitative Data - Data Table
Part 2
I. Graph
J. Statistics
K. Significant Figures
L. Analysis of Claim/Evidence/Reason (CER)
M. Possible Experimental Errors
N. Conclusion
O. Applications & Recommendations for Further Use
P. Abstract (only for State and Nationals)

Yes, this event sounds super duper simple but that’s what sets the high scorers apart from others. Knowing the sections by heart, memorizing the rubric bullets, dividing time up properly, and taking the test with a mindset to maximize your points! 
Here is the link to the SOINC website, which has all the links put up there along with other resources and practice tests. 
Happy learning…
By the way, comment something on this doc, a question or anything, for us to keep track of who read all the way down!
